text:
The ParisOlympicsis up and running after a spectacular – if soggy – opening ceremony kick-started what promises to be a fortnight of compelling action and drama.

Spectators had already been treated to plenty of memorable moments after the football, rugby sevens, handball and shooting competitions began in the week, but tonight’sParade of Nationsand the symbolic lighting of the torch marked the official start of the Games.

For the first time in Olympic history, thissummer’s opening ceremony – which kicked off the first Games in 100 years in the French capital – took place outside of the main athletics stadium.

In the early evening, a fleet of boats began carrying sportsmen and women from the 206 competing nations down a 6km stretch of the River Seine ahead of the ambitious spectacle’s crescendo at the Trocadero.

A series of co-ordinated arson attacks paralysed France’s rail networkon the eve of the ceremony but it’s believed as many as 600,000 fans flocked to the Olympics’ epicentre to witness a piece of history.

Around 6,800 of the world’s finest athletes took centre stage across the four-hour-long party, which Lady Gaga helped launch with a dazzling French cabaret performance of Renee Jeanmaire’s ‘Mon Truc en Plumes’.

But it was Canadian pop icon Celine Dionwho stole the show with a stunning public comeback to close the ceremony.

The Parade of Nations departed from the Pont d’Austerlitz and came to a halt at the Pont d’Iena, before a grand finale – set against the unmistakable backdrop of the Eiffel Tower – brought the ceremony to an end.

This summer, each and every one of the medals, whether bronze, silver or gold, will contain a unique piece of iron from the iconic landmark.

As is tradition, Greece were the first nation to enter the Parade of Nations and Team GB, led by flagbearers Tom Daley and Helen Glover, appeared in 72nd place.

Competing at his fifth Games since Beijing, gold-medal winning diver Daley revealed it was a ‘dream come true’ to be given the honour of flying the flag for Great Britain and Northern Ireland.

Legendary British rower Glover, meanwhile, said: ‘I never thought I would be an Olympian! Being part of history as a flagbearer, I haven’t really got my head round it yet.

‘Everyone I have told so far has cried, and I cried when I was told, which goes to show how much of an honour it is and how much it means.’

Among those on board the Team GB boat was Andy Murray, who said he was relishing the opportunity to soak up the atmosphere of an opening ceremony ahead of his last ever tennis tournament.

‘I’m loving it. I’ve always enjoyed coming along to the opening ceremonies, just being around all of the other athletes is brilliant,’ the veteran Scot told BBC Sport before the Team GB boat set sail.

‘And with this being my last tournament, I want to make the most of it so I wouldn’t miss tonight.

‘I’ve had amazing experiences at the Olympics, they have been the best weeks of my career. You want to make the most of it, take these opportunities to come to the opening ceremonies, spend time chatting to the other athletes.’

But it did not all go smoothly during the ceremonyas the Olympic flag was raised upside down.

On the Seine, at least one iconic athletes struggled as Serena Williams and Carl Lewisfound it tricky keeping their balance on a boat as they helped transport the Olympic torch.

title: Paris Olympics officially underway as historic opening ceremony kicks off Games | Metro News
description: The Paris Olympics is up and running after a unique opening ceremony got underway to kick-start the greatest sporting event on the planet.
